dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for a diabetic emergency.
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
The family reported a diabetic problem. The patient was alert but extremely confused, with very low blood sugar and difficulty speaking.
Let's check his mental status. He is not fully oriented now, with a GCS of 11 showing signs of possible confusion.
Your initial blood pressure is 159 over 101, with a pulse of 64 and a respiratory rate of 20. His glucose is 52 and he appears diaphoretic.
His oxygen saturation is 98%, and the EKG shows a sinus rhythm.
We are placing a 20-gauge IV in your left arm for medication administration.
A 4-lead ECG is being started to monitor his heart rhythm.
Let me check his blood pressure again quickly.
We are now administering 100 milliliters of Dextrose 10% intravenously to raise his sugar.
His blood pressure is 132 over 71. The glucose monitor is not giving a result right now.
His mental status improved after the D10. His GCS is now 15.
We've obtained a 12-lead EKG to further rule out cardiac involvement. His EKG remains in sinus rhythm but shows borderline findings like sinus bradycardia.
His glucose has risen to 174, his blood pressure is 166 over 81, and his pulse is 68. Breathing and oxygen saturation remain stable at 97%.
The patient has no reported pain at this time.
His skin is warm, pink, and diaphoretic with no signs of pallor.
The patient declined transport but accepted treatment. He is aware of the risks of refusing care and has signed a refusal form.
Based on the ePCR findings and our field impression, we followed the hypoglycemia protocol due to diabetic coma.
We assessed for pain and found the patient has no discomfort.
Since his blood sugar was so low, we monitored it after treatment and saw a significant rise to 174.
His peripheral pulses are strong and regular, and there's no sign of poor circulation.
The head and neck showed no signs of trauma, swelling, or tracheal deviation.
The mouth is clear, the nose is unobstructed, and there's no bleeding or drainage.
His chest has equal rise and fall, and we heard clear lung sounds on both sides.
No tenderness, rigidity, or guarding in the abdomen.
His arms and legs have no obvious deformity, and pulses are strong.
The EKG was reviewed again and confirmed as sinus rhythm with some borderline findings.
He is now following simple commands, showing improved mental status.
The family reported he took more insulin than normal when his blood sugar was in the high 300s.
We obtained his age of 75 and male gender from the family.
The patient has no reported allergies.
The patient takes insulin Glargine, levalbuterol, carvedilol, and lisinopril regularly.
His medical history includes type 2 diabetes and hypertension as well as a history of cardiac arrest.
We were not able to confirm when he ate last due to his altered mental state.
The patient's confusion and low sugar appear to be due to an over-insulin dose.
We will keep assessing his condition closely for any changes.
The hypoglycemia protocol was verified and carried out exactly as outlined.
The patient declined to be transported but is aware he can call 911 if needed.
We have ensured he is safe and stable before we leave.
We took his final blood pressure as 166 over 81, and his pulse is 68.
He is breathing at 20 breaths per minute and remains well-oxygenated.
His oxygen saturation is still 97% with no issues noted.
He has no pain at this time and is fully alert.
His skin remains pink and dry, with no rash or temperature changes noted.
He is following commands and is able to make eye contact.
We found his coordination and interactions are improving since the D10.
We are now clear to leave the scene with a completed refusal form.
The patient was advised to seek care if symptoms return and to check his sugar regularly.
